speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Basner, could you tell me-- let's go back for just a moment. Tell me a little bit about the bar mitzvah.

speaker: interviewee
text: The bar-- bar mitzvah took place in December, 1940. And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --sure, it would be a bigger one, a nicer one, if it would be before the Russians occupied Riga. But still, I had a bar mitzvah, and there were about-- we did it at our-- our house. And there were about 30 people-- and I went-- I learned after. And I had a droshe and the readings. And it was in a smaller school. It-- it was also on the [INAUDIBLE] on Stabu iela, but in a smaller school-- shul.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sure.

speaker: interviewee
text: A small-- a smaller shul. And so it was a bar mitzvah then. It was-- as I said already, it was the last simcha in our house.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was the ceremony in-- at home or at the shul?

speaker: interviewee
text: At the shul.

speaker: interviewer
text: Shul.

speaker: interviewee
text: The ceremony took place at the shul. But later, we had a-- a reception at home.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see. And it was a very happy time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hmm. OK. All right, let's go on to now, the Germans have come in. And your mother was taken to the police station, where she saw very upsetting things. Now, what happened next?

speaker: interviewee
text: Next, she came home the next day. They sent her to-- to work, the first-- her first work, at-- in the city. And the next day she came home, and she would go to work. So we were glad and happy that she returned home. And she told us the whole story, what she saw there, what she witnessed there. And many times, we had the Germans, Latvians, came and plundered our house. And they would beat up. You couldn't go on the street. You could go on the street, but it was very danger. Because they were going around and catching the Jewish-- the Jewish people and taking them to the Bikernieki Forest and kill them. So we lost about 8,000 people in the first-- in the first days of the German occupation. Later, father-- father went to work. A friend came, and he offered him-- there came a Jewish man. He offered him a job with the Germans, to work. And he went to work. Because everybody, if you are not working, you cannot go in the street. You cannot get your piece of bread, even, to buy. So he went to work, and mother would go to work. I would stay home with my sister and brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there--

speaker: interviewee
text: So--

speaker: interviewer
text: No school?

speaker: interviewee
text: No school, no school. After a month or less, I think it was in the end of July, there were a-- there was a decree to-- we had to register. All the Jewish people had to go in some special place and register. And they gave you a piece of paper. And you had to wear a Star of David. You didn't have the right to go on the sidewalks, only on the road, as a horse. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] You didn't have the right to buy merchandise in the stores, or food, even. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And it went on till a few months, with such a situation.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see. What did you-- what were you thinking was going to happen then? What did your family--

speaker: interviewee
text: Nobody--

speaker: interviewer
text: --think?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nobody knew what will happen. But people are always hopeful, though, that this is-- this will remain with them in such a way. But in September, we were told that everybody has to move to the ghetto. The ghetto was organized in the suburb of Riga, in a very poor neighborhood. And we are told we have to exchange our apartments for the apartments there. We tried to find an apartment. It was very hard. Because the territory where we lived, about 15,000 or 20,000 people, they have to move more than 30,000.

speaker: interviewee
text: So finally we found-- we gave two apartments. Our friend, what lived in the same house, two families, her husband was taken in the first days, so she was with two children. She gave away her apartment.

speaker: interviewee
text: We gave our apartment. And we add some stuff, whatever we could. And then she agreed to take the two apartments for one-- two small, small rooms with a kitchen.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we went-- we went to the ghetto. It wasn't allowed to take all your stuff, what you had. A policeman came, and he told you what you can take, what not. So we took our-- our stuff. We went to the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was very crowded. We lived in one-- one-- one very small room, five of us. And it was going on. So till the end of October, 1941, the ghetto was closed. There was a fence around. Latvian guards were standing there. There is a big gate, on [? Sadovnika-- ?] Sadovnikova iela. And people would go to work, forced labor, come back. And when they came back, then the whole tragedy began. They were-- they got searched coming back. There was two lines, or two rows of the Latvian guards who are beating them up. And they had to put everything, what they had in their pockets, they would put on long tables. And if something was found, they would get beaten. And many get killed, shot there, on the spot. It was crowded. It was-- we were hungry. And the men who went-- hard work.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what kind of work did your father do then?

speaker: interviewee
text: He-- he worked, actually, by his-- as-- as-- by his-- by shoes. Because they didn't have-- they didn't have specialists in this field. So they were-- they had to take the Jewish people who were working, where they choose to take them to work in the same-- the same trade.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get food?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hmm. We-- there were one store in the ghetto and-- you know, or area. And you get very little food, half the ration-- ration what the other-- the Gentile population got in the city. So they had a small-- little food, but we had even half of it. It was very, very little. And it was-- the-- the-- the food was rotten, and it was spoiled. Such things we would get.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your father try to bring in more food or not?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, he tried. But it was very danger. It was very risky. He tried.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the guards were all Latvian? Were some German?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were-- it was under the direction from Germans, but the guards were Latvian.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see. Was there running water and toilet facilities?

speaker: interviewee
text: In our place, what we got-- it depends from the house where you live. In our place, we didn't have facilities. The-- the toilet was on the-- on the-- on the yard, in the yard. And we didn't have water at home. It was a very, very poor neighborhood.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who had lived there before you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mostly Russian workers. Mostly Russian workers.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you left your original home, who did you give the apartments to?

speaker: interviewee
text: The same people who lived there, in this apartment.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, the Russian workers.

speaker: interviewee
text: We exchanged.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: They came to our apartment. We moved to their apartment.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: It must have been hard.

speaker: interviewee
text: Very hard, the life in the ghetto. But it didn't-- it didn't go on for a long time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was only for a little more than a month. In the end of November, the 28th, actually, of November, there were signs or notes on the buildings that the population of the ghetto will be resettled, and we will be taken to another place. And we have to be ready to go. And you can take only 10 kilograms with you. It was a Friday. Father didn't come from work. We were very worry. And mother told me to go and to-- to learn something, what-- what's happened. And I went to a house where another man worked in the same place. And he didn't show up also. He didn't come. So we understood then he was-- they hold him at work. But as we learned later, the Germans, where they worked, they knew what will-- will take place in the ghetto. And they want to save their workers. So they didn't let them go home. Mother didn't see more, my father. I saw him a few times later, that's all. And so we-- we-- I was the-- the oldest. I was the support of my mother. So we tried the whole night-- night, we didn't sleep. And wait-- and packed, you know, we didn't know what to take. We would take some-- some food and take again. And maybe we need some-- some stuff to take, warm dress, or whatever. And so we-- we spent a sleepless night. And the whole next day went by. And in the evening, then we heard some commotion going on-- and in the morning-- the whole night. And in the morning, I went out. I went to the fence. And also, from our window, it comes to the street. It was on [? Luther ?] Street. We went on [? Luther ?] Street, 42. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I saw the whole picture, what was going on, on the street. Because all the columns who would come with the people would go on this Luther Street. You heard yelling, weeping, shooting, screaming.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the people were actually running, because all the time they would-- only you heard, [LATVIAN], [LATVIAN], faster, faster, in Latvian. And in German, [GERMAN], [GERMAN], faster, faster. And the people will go who would fell down or what, they would they kill them, rape them. People will try it only to go, only to move.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so it went on till about 10 or 11 o'clock. At 11 o'clock, I went in-- inside, and the Latvian policeman opens the door. He was probably-- like, he looked drunk, with his pistol in his hand, screaming, yelling. [SPEAKING LATVIAN]. Aren't you out yet? I'll shoot.

speaker: interviewee
text: We moved. We went out. And they-- they formed a column of five people in a row. But we were surprised because all the people would go to the right from our house. Actually to-- to east side. And us, [? we ?] [? formed ?] with a face to west, in the other directions. And they took us to a yard on the corner, to a yard, a schoolyard, where we were staying all day long in the snow. We would lie-- lay down on the snow with the children.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they would come from time to time and offer the people who are not able to go or are weak, to go with them. They have cars or wagons, horses with wagons, to go there. Mother didn't take the risk. She thought, we'll stay here. Was it intuition? Was it she knew what was-- I don't know. But we stood-- we stayed there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And in the evening, they chased us to the building across the street. And we were-- we spent there, the whole night. We heard-- again, there was plundering, rapings going on. We heard the screaming. They came to our place. They were looking for younger girls, for some-- what you have, money or jewelry, whatever.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we spent the night there, a sleepless night. And in the morning, they told us to go to our home. But in our side, where we lived, we couldn't go.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was forbidden. So we went on the other side. And there we learned that they took to the action. They called it the first action, action number one. It took place the 29th, 30th of December, 1941, till the 1st of December.

speaker: interviewee
text: But they are very, very particular, the Germans. They have a certain time. For them, it was as a work, as a job, you know? They have the time till one o'clock. So at one o'clock, the action stopped. And it will take place again the next time. So we-- I met my-- we met my aunt with the two daughters, what they had. My uncle was taken to the small ghetto. In that time, the men were forced to-- to a place in the same ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: But a small place was fenced around. And the men were forced to go there. It was about 4,500 men went to the small ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he was there, my uncle, Uncle Chaim Gleser, and my aunt, Mina, and her two daughters, Yacha and Ida. We met them, and the uncle came. And Mother began to beg him to take me to the small ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't want to go because I felt that I am now the support for my mother with the small, two children. And my brother was very close to me. He began to-- to-- to-- to cry. And to ask me not to go. And I said, no, I'll not go. But mother, she insisted. And again, I don't know it was intuition or she knew. I think mother's heart, fill-- she felt what was going on. And she told me, go. Go. Maybe you'll-- you'll meet father, and you will be together. And I went. It wasn't easy to go there. Because they-- it was allowed to go there only for adults who were 18 and older. I was only not full 14 years old. And it was a week less than 14 years.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went with my uncle. I was a little taller than usual at that age. And there was a Latvian police standing there. And he asked me, how old are you?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said, 18. He looked at me, and he said, go. So I went to the small ghetto. And during the week, later, I went to work. But in the evening, I would go and meet my mother. And the last time I saw her, it was on  the 7 of December, 1941. There was, again, signs, again, notes-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --that the rest of the people will be taken to the other place. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Mother cried. I cried, the children, brother, sister. We hugged each other. And it was the last time I saw them. In the morning, in the night, they were taken. We didn't know in that time, but now we know they were taken to the forest of Rumbula, 12 kilometers from Riga, and killed. So in those two days, the 1st of December-- 30th and the 1st of December, the 7th and 8th of December the second time, there were about 30,000 Jewish people from the Riga Ghetto who were killed in  that forest.

speaker: interviewer
text: [SIGHS] It was possible to go from the small ghetto to the large ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Only during this week.

speaker: interviewer
text: One week?

speaker: interviewee
text: One week. It was from the 1st of December till the 8th of December, or 7th of December. So I was there two or three times. It was risky, also. But to meet your mother, you don't pay attention for the risk it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the day your mother was taken away was your birthday?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, it was the 8th of December, 1941. It was the day when I was 14 years old. So from this day on, my birthday converted in a mourning day. Till now, this-- I never celebrate the day. I'm fasting, the day, remembering them. So it's more than 50 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was life like in the small ghetto, where you and your uncle lived?

speaker: interviewee
text: The small ghetto-- as we came to the small ghetto, it was the 8th. When it was 8th of December, took place a second-- action number two. The next day, we called it later, was the 'Bloody Tuesday.' The Bloody Tuesday, they were chasing-- they were searching all the houses who lived-- who lived there, and taking everyone who was in the ghetto who wasn't working in the day. It was my-- my fate or luck. I-- I-- I went to work. And when we came back, there were again-- I don't know how many people were taken again, or-- some of them to the cemetery, the Jewish cemetery, they called. We had close to the ghetto, actually. In the ghetto, we had the-- an old Jewish cemetery. They were taken to the old Jewish cemetery and killed. Some of them were taken to the Rumbula Forest again and killed there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And these were people-- men who were working or boys who were-- men and teenagers who were working?

speaker: interviewee
text: Who were work--

speaker: interviewer
text: Some of them were taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Some of them who lived in the big ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they didn't-- some of them hided. Some of them were by accident, they were taken to the action. So they chased every house. And then they came to the small ghetto, and they took the people who were home-- it was at daytime-- who weren't at work.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they took-- children were different stories, you know? They took children. They took adults. They took everyone who was at home at that-- in that day. So not everyone in the small ghetto was a working man or a boy. There were children there?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it was-- officially, it was working people. But some smuggled in their children, a few of them. There were even a few--

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh.

speaker: interviewee
text: --a few women who dressed up as men. And they went to the ghetto. But it was only a few of them. But most, it was working-- working people, working people, men who-- who went to work.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see. Did any of those women survive from the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know exactly from the few who went to the ghetto. But some of the women survived. Because in that time, when they were taken, they took about 200 women, they took to the jail as seamstresses. And they came later when the action was gone and so they took them in a house-- and actually, it was two or three houses. And they lived there. So a few hundred's left. And some of them survived, very, very, really, very few.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your work like, when you were going out to work from the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: From the ghetto-- from the ghetto, we lived on [INAUDIBLE] Street. It was, again, crowded. We were in  a small room. We have two-- two tiers, blank beds. You know, we would put one--

speaker: interviewer
text: Bunk?

speaker: interviewee
text: --on the other, bunk beds.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we were eight people in a small room. In the morning, early in the morning, you were chased to work. Everybody has his-- his column here, his work-- work group, or whatever it's called. And we went to work. I was working in the beginning, on Smilsu iela, or [? Sand ?] Street. And I was working as a janitor. I clean the-- the street, the building. We were cutting wood, heating the oven-- oven. There-- there was the central heating. And so we were working there. And conditions, it was hard work. We would risk to get something. But you had-- you-- you would meet some people there. And we would-- when we will clean, we clean the-- the rooms and everything, we would find some-- not till the end, smoked cigarettes. We would take the tobacco. And then we would change it coming to the ghetto if it was risky to take it in. But if it was OK, you took it. You would hide it. And you would change it for either some food, or whatever, so you to survive.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they have the same system of putting the-- emptying your pockets onto a table, or something?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. There wasn't tables. There was only search. They would search, but not everyone. But it was a-- they would-- one-- one column today, another one another day. You never knew which one will be searched on that day.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who were you working for? Was it--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was the Germans. They were a soldiers' dormitory. They would come from the front. They were-- they would prepare them to go again, and so they lived there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about food and sanitation in the ghetto? What about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Food and sanitation, it depends again. We had-- it was cold. It was wintertime in that time, in the beginning, when we went in the small ghetto it was in December. It was a very, very cold winter. And sanitation was-- we didn't have toilets. It was on the-- again, on the-- in the yard. And it was frozen, everything. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Food were very little. We would go to work early in the morning, it was dark, and return when it was dark. So life was terrible.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were with your uncle, though?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I was with my uncle. I was with my uncle, Chaim. Not all the time. It was sometime when-- later, when I was working on Smilsu iela, then we were together. But later, he went-- I was taken to another place to work, and then we separated. And I-- then I was working on [INAUDIBLE], in the same building where I went to school, my same-- the school where I learn.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ah, I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, and there I was working. And heating, it was a very hard-- hard job. We were heating three stories, the building. And we were heating all the ovens. We would carry the wood in-- in the ovens, clean out the rooms. It was a very hard job. But-- so we-- we spent the whole winter there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, did you go home at night when you were working there?

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- we went-- we-- in the beginning, we want-- we would go home to the ghetto. But later, it was hard for them. They had to go every day and pick us up. And then if they needed something at night or in the evening, they couldn't help us. And they need some-- what was something to do. So for them, it were more-- more convenient to have us there.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they asked the administration of the ghetto to allow them to hold them in the working place. And they called it [GERMAN]. It means outside installations. And so we were-- we lived there in the school, where we worked. And there was many such [GERMAN] in this-- in Riga, then, in that time. And about how many people were in the group that you--

speaker: interviewee
text: There were--

speaker: interviewee
text: --were in?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a small group. We were about six people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you sleep there?

speaker: interviewee
text: We slept in the-- the cellar, or in there, where we were taking off our clothes when we went to school. So there they gave us a small room, and there we sleep-- slept.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that better or worse than being in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a little better, because you didn't have to go through the search every night. You don't have-- we had to stay-- to stay up anyway in the-- early in the morning, to begin to work. But we didn't have to go you know, from one and the other side. So it-- it was a little better than to be in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, at some point, did you go back and live in the ghetto with your uncle again?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not with my uncle. I went in the ghetto later, when they took us already. In 19-- in 19-- 1943, in spring, they took us-- in the beginning of 1943, they took us to the ghetto, back. And then they began to send us to the peat bogs. We called it [INAUDIBLE]. And then we-- there we spend the whole summer working there.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the peat bogs?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the peat bogs, in [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Stop here for a moment.

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS]
